name: Talu
classification_notes: The specimen is set entirely in a stylised serif face with strong decorative character, suggesting a display-oriented design suited to editorial and atmospheric use rather than extended body text.
summary: A display serif from Beasts of England, presented through an immersive piece of folkloric short fiction centred on the Green Man.
description: Talu is sold with an elaborate narrative specimen built around a story of hedge mazes, lost cats and folk horror, which gives a clear sense of its intended atmosphere. The typeface appears to suit literary and editorial applications where a strong, characterful serif is needed. Licensing is offered across individual and multi-user tiers starting at $32.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England present Talu alongside a darkly whimsical story of a child chasing their cat into a hedge governed by a pagan figure called the Green Man, establishing the typeface's folkloric and atmospheric character. A sample PDF is available for download.

name: Archie Brackett
classification_notes: The page provides no specimen or technical detail about the typeface's letterforms, only a fictional narrative framing the font's name; classified as display given Beasts of England's typical output and the product context.
summary: A display typeface from Beasts of England, sold at a single licence tier starting at $38, with the product page given over almost entirely to a fictional short story rather than typographic detail.
description: Beasts of England present Archie Brackett with a lengthy fictional narrative in place of conventional specimen copy, a school story about a new boy who takes the blame for a classroom mishap involving his briefcase. Technical specifications are sparse: no weights, styles or language support are listed beyond the base offering. A sample PDF is available for those wanting to assess the letterforms directly.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England introduce Archie Brackett through a short story about a new schoolboy named Archie — impeccably dressed, briefcase in hand, and oddly unflappable — whose stoic good humour is tested by bullying peers and a volcanic teacher, Miss York. The narrative stands in for conventional product copy, with a link to a sample PDF provided for those wanting to see the typeface itself.

name: Cleric
classification_notes: Cleric presents as a sturdy grotesque sans-serif with three width variants and a range of weights, following the conventions of utilitarian European grotesque design.
summary: A grotesque sans-serif family from Beasts of England spanning six weights across three widths, with a variable file included in the full family purchase.
description: Eighteen static styles cover Regular, Condensed and Wide widths, each running from Regular through to Heavy, giving the family considerable range for text and display use. The full family purchase includes a variable font file, allowing fluid interpolation across the weight and width axes. The atmospheric prose used in the specimen nods to the English folk horror tradition, setting a quietly sinister tone that suits the typeface's sturdy, ecclesiastical name.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England offer Cleric as a family of 18 styles spanning three widths and six weights, with individual styles available for purchase or the full family including a variable file. Pricing is tiered by number of users.

name: Blackbike Two
classification_notes: The font's name, imagery and foundry aesthetic suggest a heavily stylised display face with blackletter or gothic influences, consistent with Beasts of England's rough, expressive output.
summary: An updated version of a 2017 display face from Beasts of England, evoking worn matte-black machinery and faded muscle.
description: The second iteration of Beasts of England's Blackbike, originally released in 2017, carries the spirit of something repainted one too many times and left to slowly disintegrate. The foundry frames it through a personal anecdote about a neighbour's battered motorbike, and that sense of weathered gravity informs the face's character. Well-suited to editorial headers, poster work or anything that benefits from a rough, lived-in quality.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England describe Blackbike Two through a childhood memory of a neighbour's massive, clumsy-matte-black motorbike that was long past its prime but impossible to ignore. The font is an updated version of the original 2017 release, with a sample PDF available for download.

name: Friend Italic
classification_notes: Classified as an italic serif based on the name and its positioning as a standalone italic typeface; the page presents only this italic cut without reference to an upright companion.
summary: A standalone italic serif from Beasts of England, sold at a single weight and priced for individual or small-team licensing.
description: Friend Italic is offered as a single italic cut from the small British foundry Beasts of England. The page presents no upright companion, positioning this as an independent italic rather than a supplementary style. Licensing is tiered from one to ten or more users, with the entry price set at $30.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England present Friend Italic accompanied by an original short story set in the Purbeck landscape, following a crow and a sheep across a winter pasture. A sample PDF is available to download.

name: Friend Regular
classification_notes: The specimen text and overall presentation suggest a traditional text serif designed for sustained reading; the name and single-style offering point to a quiet, legible roman cut suited to body copy.
summary: A single-weight text serif from Beasts of England, presented through an original short story set in the Purbeck Hills of Dorset.
description: Friend Regular is a text serif offered in one weight, priced at £30 for a single-user licence. The foundry presents it through an extended original fable about a crow and a sheep on the Dorset coast, which serves as the specimen text. The typeface appears designed for sustained reading at body sizes, with a quiet, undemonstrative character suited to literary or editorial use.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England present Friend Regular through an original short story set among the hedgerows and sea views of the Purbeck Hills, in which a crow and a sheep form an unlikely friendship over the course of a winter. A sample PDF is available on the product page.

name: Saveloy
classification_notes: The name, the chip-shop prose specimen, and the Beasts of England context all point to a characterful display typeface; the specimen text and pricing page offer no technical classification detail, but the foundry's aesthetic strongly suggests a display serif with personality.
summary: A display typeface from Beasts of England, named after the battered chip-shop sausage and carrying that same unpretentious, distinctly British character.
description: Released by Beasts of England, Saveloy takes its name and spirit from a staple of British chip-shop culture. The foundry's specimen text leans hard into working-class English nostalgia, suggesting a typeface with warmth and personality over neutrality. Technical details are sparse on the product page, but the single-licence pricing and Beasts of England's broader catalogue point to a display face suited to editorial and branding work with a vernacular edge.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England present Saveloy with a short story set around a chip shop on Castle Lane, evoking summer holidays, lunch money and the particular pleasure of a battered sausage of questionable provenance. A sample PDF is available for download.

name: Niven
classification_notes: The specimen and style naming suggest a classical text serif with upright and italic cuts across a conventional weight range, consistent with a transitional or humanist serif category.
summary: A five-weight text serif from Beasts of England, available as individual styles or as a variable font in the full family purchase.
description: Niven spans Light to Bold across upright and italic cuts, giving ten styles in total plus a variable file bundled with the full family. The foundry's specimen leans into intimate, domestically British prose, suggesting the typeface is pitched at literary, editorial and long-form reading contexts. Licensing starts at a single user and scales to studio level.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England present Niven as a text serif available in five weights with matching italics. The full family includes a variable font file, and individual styles or complete upright and italic families can be purchased separately across multiple user tiers.

name: Acorn
classification_notes: The specimen imagery and the name suggest a clean, geometric sans character; the weight range and minimal description point to a contemporary geometric or humanist sans with no overt decorative qualities.
summary: A seven-weight geometric sans from Beasts of England, available as individual styles or as a full family with a variable font file.
description: Spanning seven weights from Thin to Bold, Acorn covers the range needed for text and display work without italic or condensed companions. The full family purchase includes a variable font file, giving designers weight flexibility in a single file. The foundry's evocative copy ties the name to the Acorn Electron home computer, suggesting a nostalgic but forward-looking sensibility behind the design.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England frame Acorn through a personal memory of the Acorn Electron home computer, evoking the early-1980s bedroom computing experience of loading games from tape and the sensory intensity of early colour graphics, presenting the typeface as something that carries that same quality of quiet, formative impact.

name: Corfe Regular
classification_notes: The specimen text and the foundry's aesthetic suggest a traditional serif with classic proportions suited to literary and editorial prose; the companion italic and formal naming conventions point toward a transitional serif design.
summary: A single-weight serif with a companion italic, sold individually and suited to literary or editorial text setting.
description: Corfe Regular is a serif typeface sold as a standalone weight with a separately available italic. The specimen leans heavily on a piece of English prose evoking Corfe Castle in Dorset, signalling the foundry's interest in place and literary culture. Includes a webfont licence and a sample PDF for review before purchase.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England offer Corfe Regular at a per-user licensing model starting from a single user, with the webfont included. A companion italic is available separately, and a sample PDF is provided for those wanting to assess it before buying.

name: Corfe Italic
classification_notes: The italic cut and the literary, unhurried tone of the specimen suggest a classical serif tradition; the companion 'regular' implies a text-oriented family designed for sustained reading.
summary: An italic serif sold as a standalone cut, paired with a separate regular weight, priced accessibly for independent and small-studio use.
description: Corfe Italic is offered as an individual purchase alongside its upright companion, suggesting a family designed with text setting in mind rather than display work. The specimen leans on a warm, literary narrative set in rural Dorset, evoking the stone and countryside of the Purbeck Hills the name references. Webfont use is included in the licence, and pricing scales across user tiers from single to ten-plus seats.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England present Corfe Italic as a standalone purchase with webfont included, available alongside the regular cut. A sample PDF is offered for those wanting a closer look before buying.

name: Newsagent
classification_notes: The typeface is presented with a name and aesthetic evoking tabloid and broadsheet newspaper headline typography, placing it firmly in the display serif tradition of British press lettering.
summary: A display serif typeface drawing on the visual language of British tabloid and broadsheet headline lettering.
description: Inspired by the typographic texture of the British press, Newsagent takes its cues from the bold, no-nonsense headline faces found across tabloids and broadsheets. The name and the atmospheric story accompanying it root it firmly in a particular strand of late-1980s British everyday life. A single-weight release priced for small teams and individual users.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England present Newsagent with an autobiographical story set in a childhood paper round, evoking early mornings, Mr Johnson's newsagent, and a run-in with a VW Golf owner that captures the spirit of a very particular time and place in British life.

name: Lovechild
classification_notes: Described as a cross between two other Beasts of England typefaces, New Forest and Room 205, suggesting a serif with transitional characteristics. The page specimen and romantic, literary tone reinforce a classical serif disposition.
summary: A serif born from crossing two existing Beasts of England typefaces, New Forest and Room 205, with broad Latin Extended language support across 485 glyphs.
description: Lovechild takes its name and concept from a deliberate hybridisation of New Forest and Room 205, two typefaces from the same foundry. With 485 glyphs including a wide range of foreign characters, it covers dozens of languages. The foundry's tone is warmly literary, positioning the face for text work with a nostalgic, personal flavour.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England describe Lovechild as the literal lovechild of New Forest and Room 205, framing it through a childhood memory of conker-collecting. It contains 485 glyphs with extensive foreign character support, making it compatible with dozens of languages.

name: Sisteron
classification_notes: Explicitly described as Jugendstil in style, placing it within the Central European branch of the Art Nouveau movement. Decorative, period-referencing letterforms suited to display use only.
summary: A Jugendstil display face with 543 glyphs including a broad set of alternates and extended language support.
description: Drawing on the decorative vocabulary of the Central European Art Nouveau movement, Sisteron is a single-weight display typeface with a richly stocked character set. The 543 glyphs include numerous alternates and foreign characters covering dozens of languages, giving it more practical range than most ornamental revival faces. Best suited to titles, packaging or editorial contexts where a period Art Nouveau aesthetic is called for.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England describe Sisteron as a display font in the Jugendstil style, containing 543 glyphs with a wide array of alternates and foreign characters, making it compatible with dozens of languages. A sample PDF is available from the product page.

name: Alder Road
classification_notes: The specimen and narrative suggest a traditional text serif with classical proportions; the page lacks technical detail but the literary, nostalgic tone and name point toward a refined text-oriented design consistent with a transitional serif subcategory.
summary: A text serif from Beasts of England, sold as a single licence tier and accompanied by an evocative personal narrative about a childhood encounter with the Queen.
description: Alder Road is a text serif from the independent British foundry Beasts of England, priced at $32 for a single-user licence with multi-user tiers available. The product page forgoes technical specimen copy in favour of a short autobiographical story set on the corner of Alder and Mossley roads, framing the typeface within a distinctly British sensibility. A sample PDF is available for review before purchase.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England present Alder Road alongside a short personal story recounting a childhood moment watching a royal procession, using the narrative to set the emotional tone for the typeface rather than offering conventional specimen copy.

name: Sandleford Regular
classification_notes: Described as a calligraphic text font with display aspirations, placing it in the calligraphic serif tradition where pen-influenced letterforms are refined for sustained reading.
summary: A calligraphic text serif designed to pair with Sandleford Italic, carrying 756 glyphs including small caps and ligatures.
description: Sandleford Regular forms one half of a two-part family alongside Sandleford Italic, together making a calligraphic text serif with ambitions toward display use. The glyph set runs to 756 characters, covering small caps, ligatures and a broad range of foreign language characters across dozens of scripts. Priced at $35 per user licence.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England describe Sandleford Regular as a complement to Sandleford Italic, the two together forming a calligraphic text font family with display font aspirations. The font ships with 756 glyphs including small caps, ligatures and wide foreign language support.

name: Sandleford Italic
classification_notes: Described as calligraphic with display aspirations, the italic's origins in handwritten forms place it firmly in the calligraphic serif tradition, complementing a regular text companion.
summary: An italic companion to Sandleford Regular, combining calligraphic warmth with enough character to function at display sizes.
description: Sandleford Italic pairs with Sandleford Regular to form a text family with display ambitions, built around calligraphic letterforms that retain energy at larger sizes. The font ships with 940 glyphs, covering small caps, ligatures and a broad range of foreign characters for multilingual use. It sits comfortably in editorial and book contexts where a companion italic needs its own presence rather than simply echoing the roman.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England describe Sandleford Italic as a complement to Sandleford Regular, together forming a calligraphic text font family with display font aspirations. With 940 glyphs including small caps, ligatures and extensive foreign character support, it is compatible with dozens of languages.

name: Jack's Maggot
classification_notes: Described as 'hairy, medieval, bawdy-ballad-jamming', suggesting a decorative display face with medieval or blackletter character, positioned as funky and irreverent rather than a strict blackletter.
summary: A medieval-flavoured display face from Beasts of England, described as funky and polarising, inspired by the spirit of an 80s folk-dance band.
description: Taking its name from an 80s folk band, this display typeface leans into a medieval, bawdy character that the foundry likens to Marmite. The accompanying copy frames it as deliberately rough-edged and festive, suited to work that wants personality over polish. It sits comfortably in poster, event and editorial contexts where a folk or rustic tone is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England describe Jack's Maggot as the funky offspring of a hairy, medieval, bawdy-ballad-jamming 80s band, and invite buyers to love it or hate it like Marmite.

name: Mrs Carter
classification_notes: Classified as script due to its flowing, calligraphic character and extensive ligature set; the back-slant is an unusual structural choice that distinguishes it from conventional italic or upright scripts.
summary: A back-slanted script with 323 glyphs, including extensive ligatures, swashes and alternates for versatile expressive use.
description: The reverse slant gives this script an unusual visual tension, leaning against the direction of reading rather than with it. A large glyph set of 323 characters includes dozens of ligatures, swashes and alternates, offering considerable variation for display and editorial contexts. The storytelling copy on the product page sets a gothic, intimate tone that suits the font's character well.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England describe Mrs Carter as a rich and nuanced back-slanted font that plays like a script but with greater versatility, featuring 323 total glyphs including dozens of ligatures, swashes and alternates.

name: New Forest
classification_notes: Described as a modern, elegant serif with art nouveau leanings, placing it within a decorative serif tradition influenced by the organic, ornamental qualities of the art nouveau movement.
summary: A two-style serif with art nouveau leanings, offered in Regular and Italic with small caps, ligatures and alternates across 853 glyphs per style.
description: An elegant serif drawing on art nouveau traditions, suited to editorial, literary or branding work where a touch of period ornament is welcome. The family ships with two cuts, Regular and Italic, each carrying small caps, small numbers, multiple ligatures and alternates. Beasts of England accompany it with a prose meditation on the New Forest itself, giving the release a distinctly literary character.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England describe New Forest as a modern, elegant font with art nouveau leanings. The purchase includes two styles, Regular and Italic, for a combined total of 853 glyphs, with each set including small caps, small numbers, multiple ligatures and alternates.

name: Room 205
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describes Room 205 as being in the neoclassical tradition, with high contrast strokes and expressive letterforms consistent with that classification.
summary: A bold, high-contrast display serif in the neoclassical tradition, offered in a single expressive weight with over 30 alternate characters and ligatures.
description: Room 205 draws on neoclassical serif conventions to produce a bold, characterful display face. Its 274-glyph set includes more than 30 alternates and ligatures, giving the typeface flexibility for headline and editorial work where variation matters. The foundry frames it with a fictional vignette set in a hotel where Tolkien was once a guest, gesturing at the literary and handcrafted sensibility behind the design.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England describe Room 205 as a bold, expressive font in the neoclassical tradition, featuring 274 glyphs including more than 30 alternate characters and ligatures. A sample PDF is available for review.

name: Rough Cut
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a gothic-style font; the sturdy letterforms and rough texture variants place it firmly in the blackletter/gothic tradition with a contemporary, approachable character.
summary: A gothic-style display typeface offered in clean and distressed variants, with alternate characters that lend it a worn, vintage character.
description: Four styles are included across two cuts: a clean version and a roughened distressed variant, each available in Regular and Italic. The rough versions simulate the aged texture of old tobacco tin lettering and printed ephemera, and 20 alternate characters plus ligatures give each style flexibility in composition. Well suited to packaging, editorial display and brand work where a robust, hand-worn quality is needed.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England describe Rough Cut as a sturdy, gothic-style font with an approachable edge. The family includes all four styles — Regular Clean, Regular Rough, Italic Clean and Italic Rough — each containing 259 glyphs with 20 alternate characters and ligatures. The typeface takes its name and character from the lettering found on vintage tobacco tins.

name: Carnaby Street
classification_notes: A condensed display typeface with a mod/retro flavour, offered in clean, rough and blowout variants. Its narrow proportions and decorative character place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A condensed display face with mod-era flavour, sold in three stylistic variants: clean, rough and blowout.
description: Inspired by the narrow, bustling character of London's Carnaby Street and its association with 1960s mod culture, this condensed display typeface comes in three variants: Clean for sharp, polished use; Rough for a worn, printed texture; and Blowout for a more distressed look. The family includes 294 glyphs with over 30 alternate characters and ligatures, giving considerable flexibility for headline and decorative work. Best suited to editorial, branding and event design where a retro British flavour is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Beasts of England describe Carnaby Street as a modern take on a classic condensed typographic style, with 294 glyphs including over 30 alternate characters and ligatures. The family is available in three styles: Clean, Rough and Blowout.
